title: Default Systemization

content.question: Is your default day actually designed, or just whatever happens?
sop: Design your ideal default day on paper, wake time, work blocks, meals, wind-down
Identify 3 non-negotiables that anchor the whole structure
Automate what you can, recurring calendar blocks, standing orders, auto-reminders, so the default doesn't rely on willpower
Rate how automated (vs. willpower-dependent) and how habitual your day currently is (see calculation, page 5)
Review monthly and adjust the design, not just your discipline
content.explainer: A schedule that only works when you feel disciplined isn't actually a system, it's just willpower wearing a calendar. The point of a designed default is that it holds its shape automatically, on the exact days motivation is lowest, because the structure doesn't need you to decide anything in the moment.
questions: Is your default day actually designed, or just whatever happens?
If you removed every calendar reminder and app nudge, would your day still run, or would it fall apart?
What's the one non-negotiable in your day that happens no matter how you feel, and what's still up for negotiation that shouldn't be?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Automation 7 (how much runs without a decision), Habit 6 (how ingrained the routine is). Consistency = 7 times 6 = 42. Higher score means your day holds its shape even on hard days; lower means the schedule only works when you're already feeling disciplined, which is exactly when you don't need it.
content.closing_statement: A good default survives the days you're not motivated.

caption:
Principle Nº 227: Default Systemization

Automated daily schedule.

Ask yourself: Is your default day actually designed, or just whatever happens?
